intro_band.agent_remit_summary: The remit governs `deepagents-cli`, a deterministic local command-line tool that scaffolds, validates, bundles, and deploys a Managed Deep Agents project onto the LangSmith platform — it performs no LLM inference itself. It exposes exactly four subcommands (`init`, `deploy`, `agents`, `mcp-servers`) and may talk only over HTTPS to `api.smith.langchain.com` (the platform API and Hub) using the developer's own `LANGSMITH_API_KEY`, plus browser-launched MCP OAuth verification URLs. Its central promises are bundle fidelity (ship only the project's declared sources, no path escapes or `.env` folding), credential hygiene (keys never persisted, logged, or printed), HTTPS-only transport, confirmation gates before updating or deleting remote resources, and never auto-registering or deploying against unregistered MCP servers.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A pure-Python 3.11+ package (`deepagents_cli`, ~2,900 LoC) with an argparse entrypoint in `main.py` that wires only `init`/`deploy`/`agents`/`mcp-servers` and redirects bare invocations to `deepagents-code`. The deploy pipeline is cleanly separated: `project.py` parses and validates the project (rejecting symlinks and path escapes), `payload.py` builds the bundle as a pure function over declared sources, `api_client.py` is an `httpx` client with `trust_env=False` and HTTPS+userinfo enforcement, `mcp_resolver.py` validates MCP-server registration, and `state.py` persists only non-secret deploy state under `~/.deepagents/`. The package ships a committed pinned `uv.lock`, an iterated `THREAT_MODEL.md`, and a comprehensive unit-test suite including symlink/path-escape and `http://` cases. A root `.mcp.json` (docs/reference LangChain HTTP servers) is contributor dev-tooling, not read by the CLI at runtime.

behavior_summary: This is a well-engineered deterministic deploy tool whose declared controls are, with one exception, actually implemented and code-enforced rather than merely documented: path containment, HTTPS-only transport, confirmation gates, credential non-persistence, and MCP-registration validation all trace to operative code, corroborated by a real threat model and security-focused unit tests. The dominant gap is a single missing check — neither `mcp-servers add`/`update` nor the deploy resolution path validates that a remote MCP server URL uses TLS, so the remit's explicit "remote MCP URLs MUST use TLS" prohibition has no client-side enforcement point (`commands.py` `_execute_mcp_server_add`, `mcp_resolver.py`). The remaining observations are lower-severity hygiene items: an inert env-isolation helper (`resolve_env_var`) that no code path consumes, two unauthenticated remote MCP servers in the repo's dev-tooling config, and the absence of a durable audit trail for deploy and delete mutations.

rules.rule_text: Assemble the agent payload and managed-directory file tree **only** from the project's declared sources, then upsert the agent on `/v1/deepagents/agents` (create when new, patch metadata + commit Hub directory when it already exists).
Validate that every MCP server URL referenced by the project is already registered in the workspace and invocable by the caller's identity — never auto-registering servers during deploy.
Offer a `--dry-run` that prints the exact payload and directory files it would send, without contacting any mutating endpoint.
Running the deployed agent itself, or performing any LLM inference — the CLI produces the artifact and hands it to the platform; it never invokes a model.
Acting as an interactive coding assistant or REPL — that surface moved to the separate `deepagents-code` (`dcode`) package; a bare `deepagents` invocation only redirects the user there.
Default endpoint `https://api.smith.langchain.com`; scheme forced to HTTPS, userinfo rejected, proxy env ignored (`trust_env=False`).
Scaffolding writes new files; overwriting an existing project folder requires `--force`.
Writing, committing to version control, logging, or printing platform / provider / Hub / tracing credentials or MCP auth-header values.
Folding secret material (e.g. `.env` contents) into the seeded bundle payload — environment stays environment; it is never merged into the skills, subagent, or managed-directory payload.
Pulling any file into the bundle from outside the resolved project root, via symlink, path traversal, or an undeclared source.
Letting repository-local deploy state steer authenticated requests (auth resolution ignores repo-local state).

Deploying to an `agent_id` declared in `agent.json` that local state has not previously confirmed — the CLI must show the target agent name/id and require a `y/N` confirmation before updating that remote agent (bypassable only with `--yes`).
Deleting a workspace agent or MCP server (requires `y/N` confirmation or `--yes`).
Deploying a project that failed configuration validation.
Reaching the platform over a non-HTTPS endpoint, or one carrying userinfo.
Bundling, registering, or deploying a project that references a remote MCP server over a non-TLS URL (`http://` / `ws://`) — remote MCP server URLs MUST use TLS (`https` / `wss`).
Auto-registering an MCP server, or deploying while a referenced MCP server is unregistered or uninvocable by the caller's identity.
Creating a fresh agent via `--reset` while `agent.json` still declares an `agent_id`.
HTTP 5xx responses are retried once with a short backoff; a directory-commit conflict (409/412) is re-fetched and re-applied once.
Each invocation runs to completion and exits; it does not linger or poll except for the bounded post-deploy health check and OAuth session polling (with a `--timeout`).
Any outbound destination beyond the platform, Hub, tracing, and MCP-OAuth verification URLs.

findings.summary: Neither MCP-server registration nor the deploy resolution path validates that a remote MCP server URL uses TLS, so the remit's "MUST use TLS" prohibition has no client-side enforcement.
findings.description: `mcp-servers add` and `update` accept an arbitrary `--url` and forward it to the platform without checking the scheme, and the deploy-time resolver only normalizes and matches URLs, never re-checking `https`/`wss`. A developer can therefore register or deploy against an `http://` or `ws://` MCP server, which the deployed agent would later reach over cleartext carrying its auth headers. The platform may enforce TLS server-side, but no client-side gate exists.
policy_rule_text: Bundling, registering, or deploying a project that references a remote MCP server over a non-TLS URL (`http://` / `ws://`) — remote MCP server URLs MUST use TLS (`https` / `wss`).

evidence.snippet: _execute_mcp_server_add uses urlparse(args.url).hostname only for a display name and passes args.url straight to create_mcp_server with no scheme check
_normalize_url lowercases and strips a trailing slash but never validates the URL scheme; resolve_referenced_servers matches on that normalized value alone

recommended_actions: In `_execute_mcp_server_add` and `_execute_mcp_server_update` (`commands.py`), reject any `--url` whose scheme is not `https` or `wss` before calling `create_mcp_server`/`update_mcp_server`, mirroring `_normalize_endpoint`'s HTTPS enforcement.
Add the same scheme check to the deploy path in `mcp_resolver.resolve_referenced_servers` so a previously-registered non-TLS URL cannot authorize a deploy.

findings.summary: The documented `DEEPAGENTS_CLI_`-prefixed env-var isolation feature is inert — `resolve_env_var` is defined but no code path consumes it.
findings.description: `model_config.resolve_env_var` implements a `DEEPAGENTS_CLI_` prefix override so users can scope keys to the CLI without colliding with identically-named shell exports. But auth and endpoint resolution in `api_client.from_env` read `os.environ.get` directly, so the prefix override never takes effect. The advertised capability does not run; it is a declared-but-never-consulted config surface, not a security control.

evidence.snippet: resolve_env_var(name) implements the DEEPAGENTS_CLI_ prefix override; grep shows zero call sites outside this module
from_env reads os.environ.get("LANGSMITH_API_KEY")/("LANGCHAIN_API_KEY") and the *_ENDPOINT vars directly, bypassing resolve_env_var

recommended_actions: Either route `api_client.from_env`'s key and endpoint lookups through `resolve_env_var` so the documented prefix isolation actually applies, or remove `model_config.py` if the feature is no longer intended.

findings.summary: The repo-root `.mcp.json` registers two remote MCP servers with no client authentication, and their hosts are not among the remit's authorized counterparties.
findings.description: `.mcp.json` declares `docs-langchain` and `reference-langchain` as HTTP-transport MCP servers with no auth block. They pass the TLS and no-secrets bars but fail the MCP minimum-bar client-authentication check, and their hosts (`docs.langchain.com`, `reference.langchain.com`) are outside the remit's trusted-domain list. This is contributor dev-tooling loaded by editors/Claude Code, not read by the CLI at runtime, so the exposure is limited to read-only documentation lookups by a developer's own client.

evidence.snippet: mcpServers docs-langchain and reference-langchain declared as type http with https URLs and no auth/headers block

recommended_actions: Document these two servers as accepted read-only documentation endpoints (a short note in AGENTS.md or a comment in `.mcp.json`), or scope them behind auth if the servers ever move beyond public read-only docs.

findings.summary: Remote-mutating operations (deploy, agent delete, MCP-server delete) are printed to stdout but not recorded to any durable, structured audit log.
findings.description: The CLI surfaces deploy summaries and delete confirmations only to the terminal; `logging` is used for diagnostics with no file handler installed, so nothing persists after the process exits. A `--yes`-driven delete of an agent or MCP server therefore leaves no local trail of what was changed. For a supervised one-shot developer tool this is a low-severity observability gap, but a durable record of mutations would aid incident reconstruction.

evidence.snippet: agents delete calls client.delete_agent then prints "Deleted {id}" to stdout; no structured/durable record of the mutation is written
_print_deploy_result prints the deploy summary to stdout only; no action-level log entry is persisted

recommended_actions: Append a structured record (timestamp, operation, target id, revision) for each deploy/delete mutation to a rotating log under `~/.deepagents/`, so mutations are reconstructable after the process exits.

positives.title: Bundle path containment (symlink + traversal rejection)
positives.description: The project parser rejects symlinks and any path that resolves outside the project root, enforcing the CLI's core bundle-fidelity promise deterministically rather than by convention.

positives.title: HTTPS-only transport with userinfo rejection and no proxy-env influence
positives.description: The API client forces an HTTPS scheme, rejects embedded userinfo, and sets trust_env=False so proxy/TLS environment variables cannot redirect authenticated requests.

positives.title: Confirmation gates on remote update and delete
positives.description: Deploying to an agent_id declared in agent.json requires an interactive y/N confirmation on first use, and deleting an agent or MCP server is gated the same way (bypassable only with the documented --yes).

positives.title: Credentials never persisted; MCP header values redacted
positives.description: Deploy state stores only agent_id, revision, and a URL-to-id map — never API keys — and mcp-servers get redacts header values to ***, matching the threat model's DC1 "process environment only" classification.

positives.title: Committed pinned lockfile with dependency-drift CI
positives.description: A committed uv.lock pins all 312 resolved packages beneath the pyproject ranges, and Dependabot plus check_lockfiles.yml / check_release_deps.yml gate drift in CI.

positives.title: Iterated threat model backed by security unit tests
positives.description: THREAT_MODEL.md is a real, revision-tracked adversarial analysis whose findings map to shipped controls, corroborated by unit tests that exercise symlink/path-escape and non-TLS-URL cases.

log_files.no_logs_note: The package emits diagnostics to stderr and via logging.getLogger but installs no file handler, so no durable log file is written to disk; the absence of an action-level audit trail for deploy/delete mutations is reflected in the Monitor Continuously score and documented in finding PRAX-2026-07-17-004.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Established. The tool has coherent, code-level controls across most of the framework — narrow code-enforced command surface, strict input/path validation, hardened HTTPS transport, approval gates on remote mutations, a committed pinned lockfile, and a genuine threat-model-plus-tests security practice. It is held back from a higher score by a real transport gap (no TLS enforcement on MCP server URLs) sitting inside an otherwise-strong Zero Trust posture, and by thin runtime observability — diagnostics reach stderr but no durable action-level audit log records what was deployed or deleted.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The command surface is narrow and enforced in code — `main.py._dispatch_command` wires only `init`/`deploy`/`agents`/`mcp-servers` and redirects any bare invocation, and the forbidden REPL/inference/dev-server surfaces are genuinely absent; the only blemish is the inert `resolve_env_var` helper.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The CLI performs no inference, and the bundle it produces is a pure function over the project's declared sources (`payload.py`) with no `.env` or secret folding and project-`.env` proxy/TLS keys blocked (`config.py`); the only inbound-data note is two unauthenticated MCP doc servers in the repo's dev-tooling `.mcp.json`.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Strong operative controls — symlink/path-escape rejection in `project.py`, HTTPS+userinfo+`trust_env=False` enforcement in `api_client.py`, confirmation gates on remote update/delete, and repo-local state ignored for auth — are undercut by one real gap: no code validates that MCP server URLs use TLS before registration or deploy.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: A committed pinned `uv.lock` (312 packages) backs the floor-with-ceiling ranges in `pyproject.toml`, Dependabot covers `uv` and github-actions across all libs, and `check_lockfiles.yml`/`check_release_deps.yml` gate dependency drift in CI; no credentials live in the workspace.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: `THREAT_MODEL.md` is a genuine iterated analysis (data classification, threat register, investigated-and-dismissed log, revision history) whose findings trace to shipped controls, reinforced by security unit tests (symlink/path-escape/`http://`); it is capped at Established by a documented staleness — the body predates the 0.1.0 REPL split and is disclaimed as auto-generated.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Logging infrastructure exists (`logging.getLogger`, stderr diagnostics) but there is no durable, structured, action-level record — deploy and delete mutations are printed to stdout only, so a `--yes`-driven delete leaves no local audit trail.
